channel.title: IT-Security & Privacy News Aggregator
channel.description: Aggregierter RSS-Feed aus führenden IT-Security, Privacy und Cyber Policy Quellen

item.title: Can Clothes Make You Invisible to Facial Recognition?
item.description: Does life feel Orwellian sometimes? One researcher has a solution for you: graphic tees that confuse the neural networks in surveillance cameras.
item.dc:creator: Nate Nelson

item.title: Iran, Russia, China Target Water Systems for Sabotage
item.description: Nation-state attackers breach water systems through weak passwords, exposed PLCs, and poor segmentation — not sophisticated malware.
item.dc:creator: Alexander Culafi

item.title: Malicious Perplexity Chrome Extension Intercepted Searches and Address Bar Input
item.description:
Microsoft has found a malicious Chrome extension that posed as the AI search engine Perplexity and quietly logged what people searched for. It routed every query and every character typed into the address bar through an attacker-controlled server before redirecting users to real results.
Microsoft says Google removed it from the store after responsible disclosure. The extension was called "
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: WhatsApp is Finally Getting Usernames to Help Keep Phone Numbers Private
item.description:
WhatsApp on Monday officially announced the start of global reservations of usernames with an aim to protect the privacy of more than three billion users on the messaging platform.
The optional feature is designed to help users connect with someone on the service through usernames, as opposed to directly sharing their phone numbers. Username reservations will start rolling out starting today,
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Factoring RSA Keys with Many Zeros
item.description: <p>Interesting research on a <a href="https://blog.trailofbits.com/2026/06/12/factoring-short-sleeve-rsa-keys-with-polynomials/">new class</a> of weak RSA keys: keys with lots of zeros. It turns out that these keys are out in the wild.</p> <blockquote><p>The badkeys project is an open-source service that checks public keys for known vulnerabilities. While developing this tool, Hanno collected a massive number of real-world keys from public sources, including Certificate Transparency logs, internet-wide TLS and SSH scans, PGP keys, and many others. By searching this dataset for unexpectedly sparse RSA moduli, we uncovered a large number of keys in the wild with the patterns in Figure 1...</p></blockquote>
item.dc:creator: Bruce Schneier

item.title: WhatsApp Rolling Out Username Feature to Bolster Phone Number Privacy
item.description: <p>An optional ‘username key’ adds another layer by requiring a secondary credential before someone can message users.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.securityweek.com/whatsapp-rolling-out-username-feature-to-bolster-phone-number-privacy/">WhatsApp Rolling Out Username Feature to Bolster Phone Number Privacy</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.securityweek.com">SecurityWeek</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Eduard Kovacs

item.title: Mustang Panda Uses Zoho WorkDrive as Command Channel in Indian Government Attacks
item.description:
The China-aligned espionage group&nbsp;Mustang Panda&nbsp;is running two campaigns against the Indian government and hydropower targets, deploying new malware and turning a legitimate cloud service into its command channel.
Acronis Threat Research Unit&nbsp;found active compromises inside Indian government networks, including machines used by senior administrative staff, and worked with&nbsp;
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: OpenAI Reveals GPT-5.6 Sol Cybersecurity Model, Restricts Early Access
item.description: OpenAI is previewing its GPT-5.6 Sol model to a vetted few at the US government's request

item.title: ⚡ Weekly Recap: Linux Kernel Flaws, AI Malware Tricks, Turla Backdoor, Infostealers and More
item.description:
This week was a reminder that attackers do not always need big tricks. One small mistake, one old access path, one missed patch, and suddenly the door is open.
The noise is not all noise, either. Forums are talking, researchers are finding easy cracks, and defenders have more cleanup waiting.
Here’s the full Monday recap.
⚡ Threat of the Week
New DirtyClone Linux Kernel Flaw Lets Local
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Telegram-Based Millenium RAT Campaign Infects 60,000 Devices
item.description: Group-IB says Millenium RAT, now rewritten in C++, has hit 62,289 devices in 160+ countries

item.title: Researchers Demo New Claude Code Attack Using Harmless-Looking Repositories to Hijack Developer Machines
item.description: <p>Indirect prompts hidden in a repository can lead to Claude Code spawning a reverse shell on the developer’s machine.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.securityweek.com/new-attack-abuses-claude-code-and-harmless-looking-repositories-to-hijack-developer-machines/">Researchers Demo New Claude Code Attack Using Harmless-Looking Repositories to Hijack Developer Machines</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.securityweek.com">SecurityWeek</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Ionut Arghire

item.title: The AI Exchange: Innovators in Payment Security Featuring Utimaco
item.dc:creator: amalone@pcisecuritystandards.org (Alicia Malone)

item.title: Straiker Raises $64 Million for AI Security Platform
item.description: <p>The startup’s platform can identify AI agents and provide visibility into their access, behavior, and risks.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.securityweek.com/straiker-raises-64-million-for-ai-security-platform/">Straiker Raises $64 Million for AI Security Platform</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.securityweek.com">SecurityWeek</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Ionut Arghire

item.title: Insurance Regulators Group NAIC Hit in Oracle PeopleSoft Hack
item.description: <p>The ShinyHunters extortion group claims to have stolen 3.1 TB of data from the organization.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.securityweek.com/insurance-regulators-group-naic-hit-in-oracle-peoplesoft-hack/">Insurance Regulators Group NAIC Hit in Oracle PeopleSoft Hack</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.securityweek.com">SecurityWeek</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Eduard Kovacs

item.title: 236,000 DCloud Uni-App Sites Used in Crypto Scams, Phishing, and Wallet Drainers
item.description:
New findings unearthed by Infoblox show that more than 236,000 websites are using investment scam templates built using a legitimate Chinese open-source, cross-platform application development framework called DCloud Uni-App.
The templates power bogus cryptocurrency exchanges, multi-language pig-butchering operations, WhatsApp phishing networks, fake gambling platforms, brand-impersonation
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Amazon Q VS Extension Flaw Leads to Cloud Credential Theft
item.description: Adversaries could plant a malicious repository that can execute arbitrary code and steal cloud credentials by exploiting the vulnerability, which showcases growing MCP risk.
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth Montalbano

item.title: Why Post-Quantum Cryptography Starts With Credentials
item.description: Today’s encrypted data, such as credentials, may no longer remain confidential in the future because the public-key cryptography protecting it will soon be broken by quantum computers. Although no machine today can break elliptic curve cryptography or RSA, quantum hardware is advancing rapidly and will inevitably change how organizations protect their data. Ciphertext and credentials captured by
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Gamaredon Expands Ukraine Attacks with New Malware and Cloud Service Abuse
item.description:
A Russian advanced persistent threat (APT) group has continued to evolve and expand its malware arsenal as part of its ongoing cyber onslaught against Ukraine throughout 2025.
Slovakian cybersecurity company ESET said it observed 35 distinct spear-phishing campaigns mounted by Gamaredon against new targets, with most of them taking place in the second half of the year. Primary targets of these
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: ‘DirtyClone’ Linux Kernel Vulnerability Leads to Root Access
item.description: <p>A variant of DirtyFrag, the flaw allows unprivileged local users to manipulate the Linux page cache and gain root privileges.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.securityweek.com/dirtyclone-linux-kernel-vulnerability-leads-to-root-access/">&#8216;DirtyClone&#8217; Linux Kernel Vulnerability Leads to Root Access</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.securityweek.com">SecurityWeek</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Ionut Arghire

item.title: Robot Police Officers
item.description: <p>We&#8217;ve taken one small step towards robot police officers: a drone capable of disarming a suspect:</p> <blockquote><p>In a <a href="https://www.instagram.com/reel/DZ4-tdPtbey/">June 22 video</a> posted on the Sacramento County Sheriff’s Office’s Instagram page, an officer wearing goggles can be seen operating a drone to retrieve a knife from an armed suspect hiding inside a cluttered house. &#8220;After not responding to negotiators, a drone was deployed inside the residence,&#8221; the post says. &#8220;Drone pilots located the suspect hiding in a corner of a garage&#8221; and then used a high-powered magnet attached to the drone to grab the knife out of the suspect&#8217;s hand. In the video ­ which is soundtracked by the &#8220;Mission: Impossible&#8221; theme song&#8212;the intercepted knife can be seen spinning around in the air as the drone carries it back to the deputies...</p></blockquote>
item.dc:creator: Bruce Schneier

item.title: OpenAI and Anthropic Limit New AI Models to Trump-Approved Customers During Cybersecurity Review
item.description: <p>ChatGPT maker OpenAI said Friday it is restricting the release of its new artificial intelligence model at the request of President Donald Trump’s administration.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.securityweek.com/openai-and-anthropic-limit-new-ai-models-to-trump-approved-customers-during-cybersecurity-review/">OpenAI and Anthropic Limit New AI Models to Trump-Approved Customers During Cybersecurity Review</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.securityweek.com">SecurityWeek</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Associated Press

item.title: US Federal Insurance Regulator Confirms Data Breach Via Oracle Flaw
item.description: An attacker has exploited a zero day in Oracle Peoplesoft to gain access to the IT systems of the NAIC, the standard-setting association for the US federal insurance system

item.title: US Offers $10 Million Bounty for Russian State Hackers as Messaging App Attacks Evolve
item.description: <p>UNC5792 and UNC4221 have been targeting US government officials, military leaders, and allied personnel.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.securityweek.com/us-offers-10-million-bounty-for-russian-state-hackers-as-messaging-app-attacks-evolve/">US Offers $10 Million Bounty for Russian State Hackers as Messaging App Attacks Evolve</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.securityweek.com">SecurityWeek</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Ionut Arghire

item.title: Russian Hackers Accused of Destructive Cyber-Attack on Jaguar Land Rover
item.description: Experts warn the Jaguar Land Rover breach bears hallmarks of Kremlin-backed hackers, citing novel ransomware, strategic timing and efforts to obscure attribution

item.title: Microsoft Removes 119 Edge Extensions That Hid Malware in Images and Fonts
item.description:
Microsoft has shut down a long-running malicious extension operation on the Edge Add-ons store that hid its payloads inside ordinary image and font files, then woke up days after install to steal credentials and run ad fraud.
The company calls it&nbsp;StegoAd, a mash-up of steganography and adware, and ties 119 extensions to a single threat actor it says has been active since at least 2021.
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: FBI Sounds Alarm Over Russian Intelligence Signal Phishing
item.description: The FBI claims Russian spies are targeting Signal backup keys

item.title: OpenAI Unveils GPT-5.6 Sol as Its Most Advanced Cybersecurity AI
item.description: <p>The company says Sol matches competing systems like Mythos Preview while using only a third of the output tokens.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.securityweek.com/openai-unveils-gpt-5-6-sol-as-its-most-advanced-cybersecurity-ai/">OpenAI Unveils GPT-5.6 Sol as Its Most Advanced Cybersecurity AI</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.securityweek.com">SecurityWeek</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Eduard Kovacs

item.title: Public PoC Released for Critical libssh2 CVE-2026-55200 Client-Side SSH Flaw
item.description:
A public proof-of-concept is now out for CVE-2026-55200, a critical flaw in libssh2 that lets a malicious or compromised SSH server trigger memory corruption on a connecting client, with possible code execution. No credentials, no user interaction. The bug affects every release up to and including 1.11.1 and carries a CVSS 4.0 score of 9.2.
libssh2 is a client-side SSH library, not a server.
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Hijacked npm and Go Packages Use VS Code Tasks to Deploy Python Infostealer
item.description:
Cybersecurity researchers have uncovered two hijacked npm packages and a cluster of Go packages that are designed to deploy a Python-based information stealer on compromised Windows, Linux, and macOS hosts.
"This attack avoids the most common npm execution paths through lifecycle scripts, perhaps in an attempt to remain 'compatible' with npm v12's security hardenings," JFrog said in a
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Top 25 Cyberattacks in Sports: Does Defense Win Championships? | UpGuard
item.description: From malicious insider espionage to devastating double-extortion campaigns, here are the top 25 cyberattacks in sports history.

item.title: Ukraine Says Russian Intelligence Used Fake Support Texts to Steal Messaging Credentials
item.description:
The Security Service of Ukraine (SSU) said it, together with the U.S. Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI), uncovered a long-running campaign orchestrated by Russian intelligence services to break into the messaging accounts of government officials, military personnel, politicians, and activists in Ukraine, Europe, and the U.S.
The systematic cyber attacks aimed at stealing sensitive
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: OpenAI Previews GPT-5.6 Sol With Restricted Access and Stronger Cyber Safeguards
item.description:
OpenAI on Friday released three versions of GPT-5.6, called Sol, Terra, and Luna, as a limited preview to a small number of companies as part of an ongoing engagement with the U.S. government.
While Sol is the latest flagship model and the most powerful, Terra strikes a balance between efficiency and power, and Luna is fine-tuned for speed and affordability.
"GPT‑5.6 Sol launches with our most
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Chinese Framework Powers 200,000 Scam Sites
item.description: <p>Threat actors are selling investment scam templates created using the legitimate DCloud Uni-App toolkit.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.securityweek.com/chinese-framework-powers-200000-scam-sites/">Chinese Framework Powers 200,000 Scam Sites</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.securityweek.com">SecurityWeek</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Ionut Arghire

item.title: Third-Party Breaches Teach Education Sector a Costly Lesson in Vendor Risk
item.description: Rising threats from third-party actors are forcing institutions to play defense to protect student data from ransomware and other attacks.
item.dc:creator: Bree Fowler

item.title: The Chinese Control the Majority of Argentina’s Squid Fleet
item.description: <p>Chinese companies control nearly <a href="https://www.seafoodsource.com/news/environment-sustainability/chinese-companies-control-nearly-two-thirds-of-argentina-s-own-squid-fleet-according-to-new-iuu-expos">two-thirds</a> of Argentina’s own squid fleet.</p>
item.dc:creator: Bruce Schneier

item.title: FBI Warns Russian Intelligence Hackers Target Signal Backup Recovery Keys
item.description:
The FBI and CISA have updated&nbsp;their March warning&nbsp;about Russian intelligence phishing Signal accounts, and the operators have added a step: they now coax targets into handing over their Signal Backup Recovery Key.
Hand it over once, and the attacker can restore the account's backup, read the private and group message history, and take over the account. Worse, the key keeps working.
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: AI Decline? Confidence in Autonomous Penetration Testing Falls
item.description: Companies are still experimenting with automated AI systems to find security weaknesses, but fewer are relying on the technology.
item.dc:creator: Robert Lemos

item.title: New SharkLoader Malware Deploys Cobalt Strike in StrikeShark Cyberattacks
item.description:
A newly discovered cyber attack campaign has been observed delivering a previously undocumented malware family called SharkLoader that acts as a loader for deploying Cobalt Strike Beacon on compromised hosts.
Kaspersky, which is tracking the activity under the moniker StrikeShark, said the campaign has targeted a diplomatic organization in Indonesia, government organizations in Taiwan,
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Cisco Adds NHI to Security Stack With Astrix, WideField Acquisitions
item.description: Cisco joins a growing list of security platform providers that are betting that securing the agentic workforce means turning identity into the primary control plane.
item.dc:creator: Jeffrey Schwartz

item.title: Hackers Claim French Employment Leak Exposes Over 1M Records, Health Data
item.description: <p>Hackers claim 1M+ records tied to French employment apps were exposed, including HR files, health data, worker details, and plaintext passwords.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com/article/news-akaolife-data-claim-emea-france/">Hackers Claim French Employment Leak Exposes Over 1M Records, Health Data</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com">TechRepublic</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Liz Ticong

item.title: New License Plate Reader Tech Could Track Phones, AirPods, and Smartwatches
item.description: <p>Leonardo’s SignalTrace adds wireless device detection to ALPR systems, raising new questions about roadside surveillance, privacy, and security.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com/article/news-leonardo-signaltrace-alpr-device-tracking/">New License Plate Reader Tech Could Track Phones, AirPods, and Smartwatches</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com">TechRepublic</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Joseph Ofonagoro

item.title: Meta Is Testing Facial Recognition for Police and Military
item.description: <p>We know that ICE wants to <a href="https://futurism.com/artificial-intelligence/ice-facial-surveillance-glasses">deploy</a> eyeglasses with facial recognition that can identify people in real time.</p> <p>Turns out Meta is <a href="https://www.wired.com/story/meta-rank-one-computing-face-recognition-smart-glasses/">prototyping</a> the feature with a Pentagon supplier. (Alternate <a href="https://gizmodo.com/meta-is-testing-police-surveillance-tech-for-its-smart-glasses-2000771931">news</a> story.)</p>
item.dc:creator: Bruce Schneier

item.title: New Initiative Tackles Security for End-of-Life Open Source Software
item.description: The Open Source Sustainability Initiative's goal is to help enterprises manage and secure aging open source projects while maintaining regulatory compliance.
item.dc:creator: Arielle Waldman

item.title: Chinese-Speaking APT Deploys New TinyRCT Backdoor in Southeast Asia Campaign
item.description:
A Chinese-speaking advanced persistent threat (APT) actor has been linked to a new custom backdoor called TinyRCT as part of cyber attacks aimed at government entities and critical infrastructure in Southeast Asia.
The activity, particularly aimed at state-owned enterprises in the energy and government sectors, has been attributed to a threat actor called CL-STA-1062, which Palo Alto Networks
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: AI Won't Wipe-Out Entry-Level Cybersecurity Jobs
item.description: Instead of eliminating jobs for early-career cyber pros, AI is creating new opportunities for candidates with strong human decision-making skills.
item.dc:creator: Jon France

item.title: Amazon Q Flaw Enabled Cloud Credential Theft via Malicious Repositories
item.description: <p>AWS has patched the vulnerability and published its own advisory to inform customers about the potential impact. </p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.securityweek.com/amazon-q-flaw-enabled-cloud-credential-theft-via-malicious-repositories/">Amazon Q Flaw Enabled Cloud Credential Theft via Malicious Repositories</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.securityweek.com">SecurityWeek</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Eduard Kovacs

item.title: Chinese AI Models Challenge OpenAI and Anthropic on Cost and Enterprise Risk
item.description: <p>Chinese AI models are undercutting OpenAI and Anthropic on price, but enterprise adoption depends on security, compliance, and data-governance trade-offs.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com/article/news-chinese-ai-models-cost-risk/">Chinese AI Models Challenge OpenAI and Anthropic on Cost and Enterprise Risk</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com">TechRepublic</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: TechRepublic Staff

item.title: AI Upgrades, Security Breaches, and Industry Shakeups Define the Week in Tech
item.description: <p>See what you missed in Daily Tech Insider from June 22–26.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com/article/ai-upgrades-security-breaches-and-industry-shakeups-define-the-week-in-tech-2/">AI Upgrades, Security Breaches, and Industry Shakeups Define the Week in Tech</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com">TechRepublic</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: TechRepublic Staff

item.title: New Linux pedit COW Exploit Enables Root Access by Poisoning Cached Binaries
item.description:
A flaw in the Linux kernel's traffic-control subsystem can let a local unprivileged user gain root on affected systems.
CVE-2026-46331, nicknamed "pedit COW," is an out-of-bounds write in the packet-editing action (act_pedit) that corrupts shared page-cache memory. A&nbsp;public, working exploit&nbsp;appeared within a day of the CVE assignment on June 16. Red Hat&nbsp;rates the flaw as
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Amazon Q Developer Flaw Could Let Malicious Repos Run Code via MCP Configs
item.description:
A high-severity flaw in Amazon Q Developer let a malicious repository run commands and steal a developer's cloud credentials. The path was short: a developer opens the repo, trusts the workspace, and Amazon Q does the rest. Amazon has patched it.
Tracked as&nbsp;CVE-2026-12957&nbsp;(CVSS 8.5), the bug sat in how Amazon's AI coding assistant handled Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers.
Wiz
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Meeting Trump's 2030 Quantum Deadline Will be Expensive, Complex
item.description: Getting accurate visibility into IT and OT systems will be compounded by multivendor environments, misaligned update life cycles, and interoperability gaps.
item.dc:creator: Alexander Culafi

item.title: Thanks for Crushing the Submissions Inbox. We're Trying to Keep Up
item.description: It might be taking a bit longer than usual to respond to your submissions — here's why.
item.dc:creator: Becky Bracken

item.title: CISA Adds Exploited PTC Windchill RCE Flaw to KEV as Web Shell Attacks Continue
item.description:
The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) on Thursday added a critical remote code execution vulnerability impacting PTC Windchill PDMlink and PTC FlexPLM enterprise Product Data Management (PDM) and Product Lifecycle Management (PLM) software to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog, citing evidence of active exploitation.
The vulnerability in question is
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Five Eyes Warns AI Could Speed Cyberattacks Within Months
item.description: <p>Five Eyes agencies warned that AI could speed cyberattacks within months, raising new risks around prompt injection, phishing, and enterprise AI tools.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com/article/news-five-eyes-ai-cyberattacks/">Five Eyes Warns AI Could Speed Cyberattacks Within Months</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com">TechRepublic</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: TechRepublic Staff

item.title: New DirtyClone Linux Kernel Flaw Lets Local Users Gain Root via Cloned Packets
item.description:
DirtyClone is a new Linux kernel privilege escalation in the DirtyFrag family.&nbsp;JFrog Security Research published a working exploit walkthrough&nbsp;for the flaw on June 25, the first public demonstration for this variant.
Tracked as&nbsp;CVE-2026-43503&nbsp;(CVSS 8.8), it lets a local user corrupt file-backed memory through a cloned network packet and gain root. The patch landed in
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Guardian Agents: The Next Layer of Identity Governance
item.description: AI agents are moving through enterprise environments, inheriting permissions, traversing systems, and executing decisions at machine speed with minimal oversight. The identity infrastructure built to govern human access wasn't designed for autonomous actors, and the gap between what enterprises are deploying and what their governance programs actually cover is widening fast. This guide breaks
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Miasma Malware Targets npm Packages and GitHub Actions in Supply Chain Attack
item.description:
Cybersecurity researchers have flagged yet another evolution of the supply chain attack linked to the Mini Shai-Hulud, Miasma, and Hades malware family that has compromised a new set of npm packages, even as it has propagated to the Go ecosystem.
"The latest activity includes malicious npm releases affecting LeoPlatform and RStreams packages, GitHub Actions workflow abuse, and a related Go
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: One Million Passports Leaked Online
item.description: <p>A database of almost a million passports from around the world was <a href="https://cambridgeanalytica.org/data-breaches-scandals/passports-driver-licenses-exposed-public-internet-2026-51096/">leaked</a> online.</p> <p>Note what happened. A high-value credential&#8212;a passport&#8212;was used in an ancillary low-value authentication system: ID verification for cannabis dispensaries. And it&#8217;s the low-value system that got hacked, putting the high-value credential at risk.</p>
item.dc:creator: Bruce Schneier

item.title: China-Linked Hackers Strike Asian Critical Infrastructure with TinyRCT Backdoor
item.description: A China-linked threat group has been targeting critical infrastructure in Southeast Asia with a new custom backdoor called TinyRCT

item.title: Microsoft Warns of Photo ZIP Phishing Campaign Targeting Hotels with Node.js Implant
item.description:
An active phishing campaign has been targeting hotel and other hospitality organizations across Europe and Asia since April 2026, using photo-themed ZIP files to drop a Node.js implant and dig into front-desk machines, Microsoft says.
The company has&nbsp;not attributed&nbsp;the activity to a known threat actor, and the operators' end goal is still unclear.
The lure plays to how hotels work.
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: SMB cyber readiness: the road to resilience starts here
item.description: Your business may be small, but its attack surface is anything but. Readiness is the first step to resilience.

item.title: Russia Used Cellebrite on Jailed Activist's iPhone Months After Sales Cutoff
item.description:
Russian authorities used Cellebrite's UFED forensic tools to break into the iPhone of detained opposition activist Andrey Pivovarov in June 2021, three months after Cellebrite said it would stop selling its tools and services to Russia and Belarus.
The finding, published&nbsp;June 25 by the Citizen Lab, rests on two things that rarely line up: traces on the phone itself and an official Russian
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: CMC Releases Analysis and Guidance for Education Sector After Canvas Data Breach
item.description: The UK Cyber Monitoring Centre reviews the Canvas breach affecting 160 UK universities, highlighting data theft risks and financial impacts of cyber incidents

item.title: Google Details Turla's New STOCKSTAY Backdoor Used in Ukraine Espionage Attacks
item.description:
The Russian state-sponsored threat actor known as Turla has been attributed to a previously undocumented .NET backdoor called STOCKSTAY that has been deployed against government and military organizations in Ukraine, and entities that have an interest in Italian foreign policy.
Describing the Windows backdoor as continually developed by the hacking group, Google Threat Intelligence Group (
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Robinhood Cuts Access Approval Time to Support High-Velocity Development
item.description: The fintech company's engineering-first application security team reengineered the process for granting system access, making it easier and more secure for developers working on their projects. Here are the lessons learned from Robinhood's experience.
item.dc:creator: Ericka Chickowski

item.title: In Less Than 24 Hours, Attackers Weaponize Cisco CUCM Flaw
item.description: The flaw enables server-side request forgery (SSRF) and escalates privileges to root, impacting Cisco Unified CM and Unified CM SME deployments.
item.dc:creator: Jai Vijayan

item.title: Coffee with the Council Podcast: Meet This Year’s North America Community Meeting Keynote Speaker, Sharon Gai
item.dc:creator: amalone@pcisecuritystandards.org (Alicia Malone)

item.title: Russian APT 'Gamaredon' Upgrades Its Arsenal, Requiring New Defenses
item.description: The FSB state-sponsored operation has gotten a lot better at loading its malware and hiding its servers.
item.dc:creator: Nate Nelson

item.title: EdTech Attackers Shift From Schools to Their Software Suppliers
item.description: Educational institutions, the edtech companies they rely on, and, more concerningly, the challenges they pose for schools are the focus of the latest Reporters' Notebook video series.
item.dc:creator: Arielle Waldman

item.title: Local Police Collusion Hampers Crackdown on Asian Scam Centers
item.description: With tens of billions of dollars flowing into regional economies from cybercrime, scam centers continue to flourish, despite international and law-enforcement efforts.
item.dc:creator: Robert Lemos

item.title: AI and Liability
item.description: <p>Earlier this month, a German court <a href="https://the-decoder.com/landmark-german-ruling-declares-googles-ai-overviews-are-googles-own-words-and-makes-it-liable-for-false-answers/">ruled</a> that Google is liable for its AI search summaries. Rejecting defenses like &#8220;users can check for themselves,&#8221; and that they generally know &#8220;that information generated with AI should not be blindly trusted,&#8221; the court held that the AI&#8217;s summaries are reflections of the company and &#8220;above all an expression of Google&#8217;s business activities.&#8221;</p> <p>This is the latest skirmish in a decades-old battle over internet publishing. Historically, there were two different types of information distributors: carriers and publishers. A phone company is a carrier. It&#8217;ll transmit whatever you say, even discussions about committing a crime. Words are words, and the phone company does not know&#8212;nor is it liable for&#8212;the words you choose to speak. A newspaper, on the other hand, is a publisher. It decides the words it publishes, and what quotes to include in its articles. If those words or quotes are defamatory or otherwise illegal, it&#8217;s liable...</p>
item.dc:creator: Bruce Schneier

item.title: Interpol: Cybercrime Hits 30% of Recorded Crime in Surveyed APAC Countries
item.description: <p>Interpol’s latest Asia and South Pacific cybercrime assessment shows how phishing, ransomware, DDoS attacks, infostealers, and AI-enabled scams are raising security risks across APAC.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com/article/news-interpol-cybercrime-report-apac/">Interpol: Cybercrime Hits 30% of Recorded Crime in Surveyed APAC Countries</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com">TechRepublic</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: TechRepublic Staff

item.title: Cisco Vulnerability Exploited Months Before Disclosure, Google Warns
item.description: A high-severity flaw in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager disclosed in early June was exploited as early as March

item.title: Chrome Ad Blocker with 10M+ Installs Found with Dormant Script Injection Capability
item.description:
An analysis of a popular Google Chrome ad block extension for YouTube has uncovered the ability to execute arbitrary JavaScript code.
According to Island, the extension, named Adblock for YouTube (ID: cmedhionkhpnakcndndgjdbohmhepckk), has more than 10 million installs and carries a Featured badge on the Chrome Web Store.
The extension description states that it allows users to prevent web
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Europol, Microsoft Hit Malware Network Behind 27M Stolen Logins, 140,000 Infected Computers
item.description: <p>Europol and Microsoft disrupted malware infrastructure linked to 27 million stolen login credentials and 140,000 infected computers in a global cybercrime network.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com/article/news-europol-microsoft-malware-takedown-emea-eu/">Europol, Microsoft Hit Malware Network Behind 27M Stolen Logins, 140,000 Infected Computers</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com">TechRepublic</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Liz Ticong

item.title: Twenty Million US IP Connections Used by Proxy Services
item.description: Digital Citizens Alliance report claims that millions of Americans may have unwittingly had IP connections used by cybercriminals

item.title: ThreatsDay Bulletin: Smart TV Proxyware, 24-Year curl Bug, AI Crime Forums + 13 More Stories
item.description:
It’s dumb out there again.
This week has the usual smell of prod on fire and nobody wanting to admit who left the door open — old creds still working, trusted apps doing sketchy crap, browser tricks jumping the fence, and “normal” workflows turning into phishing pipes because apparently email was not enough hell already.
The worst part is how cheap some of it feels. Not elite. Not cinematic.
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Trust in Automated AI Vulnerability Scanning Collapses to 9%, New Study Finds
item.description: Cobalt study finds 20-percentage-point drop in number of organizations relying solely on AI automation for testing

item.title: New CISA Guide Helps Agencies Adopt SASE For Zero Trust
item.description: New CISA guidance shows federal agencies how to use SASE to move from legacy TIC 2.0 to zero trust

item.title: Interesting Paper Exploring Prompt Injection
item.dc:creator: Bruce Schneier

item.title: Surviving the Mythos Era: Richard Bejtlich on the Case for NDR
item.description:
Despite the abundance of telemetry at analysts’ disposal, many security operations teams struggle to answer a few basic questions during incident investigation: What happened? What evidence do we have? How do we know we’re seeing it all, in context?
Answering these questions requires teams to go beyond alerts, the most common basis for initial triage. But investigations (and their outcomes)
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: macOS Flaw Lets Standard Users Disable EDR and MDM
item.description: macos-xpc-flaw-disable-edr-mdm-standard-user-xm-cyber

item.title: Major Increase in Ransomware Attacks Targeting Europe, Warns New Report
item.description: Analysis of ransomware incidents by researchers at Black Kite found that attacks have risen by over 50% in the last year, with supply chain attacks increasing

item.title: Europe Evolves Into Ransomware's Favorite Region
item.description: After a global lull, ransomware gangs are setting sights on a rich new arena: attacking EU organizations and their suppliers.
item.dc:creator: Nate Nelson

item.title: New Gaslight macOS Malware Uses Prompt Injection to Disrupt AI-Assisted Analysis
item.description:
A previously undocumented Rust-based macOS implant and information stealer has been found to embed a prompt injection payload designed to trick a malware analyst's artificial intelligence (AI) tools and trick it into aborting or refusing an analysis of the artifact.
The malware has been codenamed Gaslight owing to this deceptive behavior. It's been assessed with high confidence that the tool is
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: New Mistic Backdoor Linked to KongTuke in ClickFix and ModeloRAT Campaigns
item.description:
A new, stealthy backdoor named Mistic has been deployed as part of suspected financially motivated attacks aimed at multiple organizations spanning insurance, education, IT, and professional services sectors since April 2026.
According to Symantec and Carbon Black's Threat Hunter Team, the backdoor, also tracked as MLTBackdoor, is said to be linked to an initial access broker (IAB) named
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Gamaredon in 2025: Leveraging tunnels, workers, dead drops, and new alliances
item.description: ESET Research analyzes Gamaredon’s new toolset and the group’s growing reliance on legitimate online services to hide its C&amp;C infrastructure and exfiltrate stolen data

item.title: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Zero-Day CVE-2026-20245 Exploited to Gain Root Access
item.description:
An unknown threat actor exploited a recently disclosed high-severity security flaw impacting Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN as a zero-day at least two months before it was publicly disclosed, according to new findings from Google-owned Mandiant.
The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-20245 (CVSS score: 7.8), allows an authenticated, local attacker to execute arbitrary commands with elevated privileges
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Attackers Hit Cisco SD-WAN Flaw 2 Months Before Disclosure
item.description: Researchers believe rogue peering was used to connect to the victim's SD-WAN devices to gain admin privileges and root-level access.
item.dc:creator: Jai Vijayan

item.title: 2026 FIFA World Cup Faces Surge in Cyber Threats
item.description: Persistent cybercrime, social engineering, and infrastructure threats continue to plague the FIFA 2026 World Cup across the US, Canada, and Mexico.
item.dc:creator: Alexander Culafi

item.title: Do CISOs Need a Code of Ethics?
item.description: Dark Reading Confidential Episode 19: Kickbacks, no-show jobs, &quot;dirty&quot; VCs, and shelf ware — industry expert Robert &quot;RSnake&quot; Hansen explains why he thinks it's time for a CISO code of ethics. It could ensure cybersecurity bosses aren't engaged in self-dealing that could risk enterprise, and even national, security.
item.dc:creator: Dark Reading Editorial Team

item.title: Healthcare Vendor Xsolis Reports Breach Affecting 1.4M People
item.description: <p>Xsolis confirmed a healthcare data breach affecting nearly 1.4 million people after a phishing attack exposed health and identity data.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com/article/news-xsolis-healthcare-data-breach/">Healthcare Vendor Xsolis Reports Breach Affecting 1.4M People</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com">TechRepublic</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Liz Ticong

item.title: Apple’s £3B iCloud Lawsuit Could Affect 40M UK Users
item.description: <p>Apple lost a bid to narrow a UK iCloud lawsuit from Which?, keeping a £3 billion competition claim on track for an October 2028 trial.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com/article/news-emea-uk-apple-icloud-lawsuit-2028-trial/">Apple’s £3B iCloud Lawsuit Could Affect 40M UK Users</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com">TechRepublic</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Joseph Ofonagoro

item.title: LastPass Confirms Vendor Breach Exposed Customer Contact, Support Data
item.description: <p>LastPass said customer contact and support data were exposed after attackers used stolen Klue OAuth tokens to access its Salesforce environment and CRM records.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com/article/news-lastpass-klue-oauth-token-salesforce-data-exposure/">LastPass Confirms Vendor Breach Exposed Customer Contact, Support Data</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com">TechRepublic</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Aminu Abdullahi

item.title: CISA Warns Critical Lantronix EDS5000 Flaw Is Being Actively Exploited
item.description:
The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) on Tuesday warned of active exploitation of a critical security flaw impacting Lantronix EDS5000 Series devices, urging Federal Civilian Executive Branch (FCEB) agencies to apply the fixes by June 26, 2026.
The vulnerability in question is CVE-2025-67038 (CVSS score: 9.8), a code injection flaw that could result in the execution
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: More Malicious OpenClaw Skills Threaten AI Supply Chain
item.description: OpenClaw removed five packages from its ClawHub skills marketplace that bypassed security checks even though they included infostealers and other threats.
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth Montalbano

item.title: Researchers Trick AI Browsers Into Leaking Credentials
item.description: LayerX tricked AI browsers including ChatGPT Atlas and Comet into bypassing their guardrails

item.title: Amadey and StealC Malware Network Disrupted, 27M Stolen Credentials Recovered
item.description:
A coordinated law enforcement operation, in partnership with private sector companies, including Bitdefender, Bitsight, ESET, and Microsoft, has resulted in the takedown of criminal infrastructure powering Amadey and StealC.
"The main common goal was to disrupt the 'assembly lines' cybercriminals use to launch ransomware, financial fraud, and attacks on critical infrastructure," Europol said in
item.dc:creator: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)

item.title: Anthropic Launches Claude Tag, Bringing AI Agents Into Slack
item.description: <p>Anthropic launched Claude Tag in Slack, giving enterprise teams an AI agent with shared context, admin controls, logs, and spend limits.</p> <p>The post <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com/article/news-anthropic-claude-tag-ai-agent-slack/">Anthropic Launches Claude Tag, Bringing AI Agents Into Slack</a> appeared first on <a href="https://www.techrepublic.com">TechRepublic</a>.</p>
item.dc:creator: Kezia Jungco

item.title: Europol-Led Operation Endgame Takes Down StealC and Amadey Infostealers
item.description: Operation Endgame seized around 50 domains and nearly 200 active IP-based servers associated with the infostealers
